statement.markdown: Given an unsorted array of integers, find the length of the longest consecutive sequence. Your function should take an array of integers as input and return the length of the longest consecutive sequence. A consecutive sequence is a sequence of numbers where each number is one more than the previous number. For example, the longest consecutive sequence in the array [100, 4, 200, 1, 3, 2] is [1, 2, 3, 4] and its length is 4.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer n, the number of elements in the array. The second line contains n space-separated integers, representing the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: A single integer representing the length of the longest consecutive sequence.
statement.notes: You can assume that the input array does not contain duplicate elements.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array

examples.explanation: The longest consecutive sequence in the array is [1, 2, 3, 4] and its length is 4.
The longest consecutive sequence in the array is [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6] and its length is 6.

hints.text: sort the input array in ascending order. this is a good starting point because we can easily find consecutive sequences in a sorted array.
use a hashmap to store the frequency of each number in the sorted array. this will help us to efficiently find the longest consecutive sequence.
the key to solving this problem is to find the longest sequence of consecutive numbers. think about how you can use the hashmap to find such a sequence.
you can use the hashmap to find the start of a sequence and then keep counting the numbers in the sequence until you find a gap. this will give you the length of the longest consecutive sequence.
the longest consecutive sequence will be the maximum length of all sequences found. you can use the hashmap to find all sequences and then return the maximum length.

editorial.title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
editorial.intuition: The problem asks for the longest consecutive sequence in an array of integers. A brute force approach would involve checking every possible sequence, which is highly inefficient. However, by utilizing a set data structure to store the array elements, we can significantly improve the efficiency of our solution.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force solution would involve generating all possible sequences and checking each one to see if it is consecutive. This approach is correct but slow, with a time complexity of O(n^3) due to the generation of sequences and checking for consecutiveness.
editorial.betterSolution: A better approach is to use a set to store the array elements, allowing us to check for the existence of an element in constant time. This observation unlocks the optimal approach.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves iterating over each element in the set. If the current element is the start of a sequence (i.e., `num - 1` is not in the set), we then check for the existence of consecutive elements. By doing so, we ensure that each element is only visited once, resulting in a time complexity of O(n).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness lies in the fact that we are checking every possible sequence start and then extending it as long as possible. Since we are using a set to store the elements, we can efficiently check for the existence of consecutive elements, ensuring that we do not miss any sequences.
editorial.dryRun: To test the solution, we can create a sample array and verify that the output is correct. For example, given the array `[100, 4, 200, 1, 3, 2]`, the output should be `4`, which is the length of the longest consecutive sequence `[1, 2, 3, 4]`.
commonMistakes: Not using a set to store the array elements, resulting in inefficient lookup times.
Not checking if the current element is the start of a sequence before extending it.
Extending the sequence unnecessarily, resulting in duplicate visits to the same elements.
Not keeping track of the longest sequence found so far.
Not handling edge cases, such as an empty array or an array with a single element.
